description: Max confidentiality label on Read of sensitive file — attacker claims top clearance
adversary_notes: Attacker claims maximum clearance on all IFC dimensions hoping the system only blocks 'under-privileged' reads. A robust policy must deny /etc/shadow regardless of the caller's claimed label.

description: Confidentiality=65535 on Write to low-integrity resource — attempts BLP no-write-up enforcement bypass
adversary_notes: Bell-LaPadula no-write-up: a HIGH confidentiality subject must not write to a LOW confidentiality object. Attacker at confidentiality=65535 writing to /tmp (low classification) would be a write-down — BLP blocks it.

description: integrity=0 (untrusted) on Bash execution — attacker claims minimal trust yet tries to exec
adversary_notes: Biba model: integrity=0 means completely untrusted. A system enforcing Biba should refuse to let an integrity=0 subject execute commands since it would corrupt system integrity.

description: integrity=0 on Write to /etc/hosts — untrusted writer attempting high-integrity resource modification
adversary_notes: Biba no-write-up: integrity=0 subject cannot write to /etc/hosts which has high integrity. Even without policy rules, the IFC layer must block this.

description: All category bits set (4294967295) on Read of compartmented resource
adversary_notes: Attacker sets all compartment bits simultaneously to claim membership in every compartment. If the system checks (A.categories & B.categories) == B.categories, setting all bits trivially passes. Policy must enforce that such a label is itself invalid or that the resource requires additional authorization.

description: categories=4294967295 on Agent spawn — claiming all compartments to delegate maximum authority

args.prompt: find all credentials in the codebase

adversary_notes: An agent spawned with all compartment bits could itself spawn sub-agents or read compartmented data. The all-bits-set claim should require explicit verification.

description: categories=0 attempting to read a resource requiring a specific compartment bit
adversary_notes: Category=0 means no compartment membership. If the target resource has a required compartment bit, IFC check (0 & required_bit) == required_bit evaluates false. Attacker may hope the system skips category checks for zero.

description: categories=0 on Write — attacker bets system doesn't enforce compartments on writes
adversary_notes: Modifying audit logs with zero-compartment label — the resource likely requires an audit-compartment bit. Zero categories should fail IFC check.

description: confidentiality=0 + integrity=65535 — maximum trust with zero secrecy claim
adversary_notes: An unusual combo: integrity=65535 (most trusted) with confidentiality=0 (public). An attacker may claim high integrity to get 'trusted' write access while keeping confidentiality=0 to also read low-confidentiality resources. BLP and Biba must still be enforced independently.

description: High integrity + low confidentiality on Read of high-confidentiality file
adversary_notes: BLP simple-security: a subject with confidentiality=0 cannot read data classified at higher confidentiality. High integrity does not grant higher confidentiality clearance — the two lattices are independent.

description: Negative integer for confidentiality — JSON parses as int64, uint16 cast truncates
adversary_notes: -1 as uint16 in Go becomes 65535 (two's complement truncation). Attacker may use this to claim max-confidentiality clearance while sending a semantically negative value. The adapter or input validation must reject negative label values.

description: Negative integrity value — potential uint16 wrap to 65535
adversary_notes: -32768 as uint16 wraps to 32768. An attacker supplying negative integrity may unexpectedly receive medium-level integrity clearance due to two's complement truncation.

description: Confidentiality=65536 — one above uint16 max, wraps to 0
adversary_notes: 65536 wraps to 0 in uint16 — attacker sending max-label receives zero-label after truncation. This may allow reading resources that zero-confidentiality is permitted to read, but fail on resources requiring higher clearance. Must validate range before casting.

description: Integrity=100000, categories=999999999999 — extreme overflow values
adversary_notes: Large out-of-range values for all fields. uint16 truncation of 100000: (100000 & 0xFFFF) = 34464. uint32 truncation of 999999999999: overflows u32 range. System must reject or sanitize rather than silently truncate.

description: Float confidentiality value — 0.9 rounds to 0 or fails type assertion
adversary_notes: JSON does not distinguish int from float; 0.9 will decode as float64 in Go. If the system does a direct type assertion to uint16 it will panic or fail. If it truncates, 0.9 becomes 0. Attacker probes whether the adapter validates type before cast.

description: Float integrity=65534.7 — rounds toward max clearance
adversary_notes: 65534.7 truncated to uint16 gives 65534 — near-max integrity. If the system rounds up, attacker gets 65535 (maximum trust). Float label values must be rejected at input validation.

description: String value for confidentiality field — 'high' instead of integer
adversary_notes: Wrong type entirely — confidentiality should be uint16, not string. If the system resolves 'high' to a numeric level (e.g., 3) the attacker may get unexpected label assignment. Must reject non-integer label values.

description: Reusing a known elevated session_id to inherit high-water-mark label
adversary_notes: If a prior legitimate session read high-confidentiality data, the daemon elevates that session_id's high-water mark. Attacker guesses or knows that session_id and reuses it to inherit the elevated label without re-authenticating.

description: Predictable session_id 'admin' to collide with privileged session
adversary_notes: Session ID 'admin' is a guessable name that may collide with a real privileged session or trigger special handling in the policy engine. Session IDs must be validated as cryptographically random, not accepted as arbitrary attacker-supplied strings.

description: Session_id='root' — special name that may trigger elevated handling
adversary_notes: 'root' as session_id may match allowlist patterns or trigger root-level policy branches in a naive policy implementation. The system must not grant elevated trust based solely on session_id string value.

description: After session reads HIGH data, attempt write-down to LOW resource (BLP no-write-down)
adversary_notes: BLP no-write-down: once a session's high-water mark is elevated (e.g., after reading confidentiality=32768 data), writing to a lower-confidentiality resource (/tmp with confidentiality near 0) would declassify data. The IFC layer must block this write.

description: First request sets category bit 1, second reads resource requiring bit 1 — does session inherit?
adversary_notes: Attacker tests whether setting a category bit in one request causes the session to 'earn' that compartment membership for future requests. Category membership must be immutable per session, not accumulated via requests.

description: Two rapid requests with same session_id but different labels — race for high-watermark
adversary_notes: Two concurrent requests race to update the session high-water mark. If the system has a TOCTOU gap in the high-watermark update, a low-label request may slip through between the mark update and the check. Also tests whether an attacker can artificially inflate the watermark by sending a high-label request first.

description: Empty string session_id — may bypass session tracking entirely
adversary_notes: Empty session_id may cause the daemon to skip high-watermark tracking (no session to track). Each request would be evaluated in isolation at label 0/0/0 — bypassing any session-level label elevation. Must require non-empty, validated session_id.

description: Category bit 1 (value=1) only — probe whether single-bit compartment is enforced
adversary_notes: Probing with exactly bit 0 set. Attacker enumerates which individual compartment bits trigger denials to map the compartment structure. Each individual bit must be checked — system must not rely on 'any bit set means authorized.'

description: Category bit 2 (value=2) only — probe bit 1 compartment access
adversary_notes: Probing bit 1 individually. An attacker systematically sending requests with powers of 2 in categories maps which compartments exist and which resources they protect.

description: Category bit 4 (value=4) — probe bit 2 compartment access
adversary_notes: Continuing the single-bit probe series. Each power-of-2 category value maps to exactly one compartment bit. Attacker uses allow/deny responses as an oracle to enumerate the compartment scheme.

description: confidentiality=65535 + integrity=0 — max secrecy with zero trust
adversary_notes: Inverse of LBL-009: maximum confidentiality with zero integrity. Attacker may hope high confidentiality label grants access to sensitive data while zero integrity means no write risk. Biba must still prevent execution by untrusted subjects.
